coredata.dc:title: Novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots with triple targetability as a fluorescent probe for bioimaging of living cells

coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jiaqing
Chen, Yu
Weng, Xiaoyu
Wang, Yiping
Qu, Junle
Song, Jun
Liu, Liwei
coredata.dc:description: Investigating the interactions between various organelles can effectively reveal the corresponding biological problems. Currently, studies of organelle interactions typically employ multiple fluorescent probes that can concurrently target different organelles. However, the simultaneous use of multiple probes is complicated to operate, and the probes can interact with each other, affecting the imaging results. Therefore, targeting multiple organelles using a single probe can enhance the process of studying organelle interactions. Carbon dots (CDs), with their abundant surface groups, are expected to solve the abovementioned problems. Herein, we successfully used m-aminophenol and ethylenediamine to prepare high anti-interference capability and triple targetability CDs (n-CDs) with hydrothermal method. The co-localization experiments with commercial probes confirmed that it can target nucleolus, mitochondria and Lysosome at the same time. The preparation of n-CDs provides a new and convenient strategy to study the interaction between various organelles for solving corresponding biological problems, thus revealing the mysteries of life.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probes
Organelle interactions
Three-target
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been partially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFF0502900); National Natural Science Foundation of China (62275166/62175161/61835009/62127819/62022059/62405201), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M712164). Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2023A1515011114/2024A1515011800/2024A1515010999), Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (JCYJ20190808160207366/JCYJ20210324095810028/JCYJ20210324095613036/JCYJ20190808151215399) and Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Photonics and Biophotonics (ZDSYS20210623092006020). We thank Instrument Analysis Center of Shenzhen University, Photonics Research Center of Shenzhen University, and Ph.D. Lin for the technical assistance with the Leica SP8 confocal microscopy instrument.

This work has been partially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFF0502900); National Natural Science Foundation of China (62275166/62175161/61835009/62127819/62022059), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M712164). We thank Instrument Analysis Center of Shenzhen University, Photonics Research Center of Shenzhen University, and Ph.D. Lin for the technical assistance with the Leica SP8 confocal microscopy instrument.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (n-CDs). (a) and (b) TEM image and size distribution of the n-CDs; (c) FT-IR spectrum of the n-CDs; (d) XPS spectrum of the n-CDs; (e) High-resolution C1s spectrum of n-CDs; and (f) High-resolution N1s spectrum of n-CDs.

Optical characterization of the n-CDs. (a) UV–Vis spectrum of the n-CDs; (b) Excitation-emission color map of the n-CDs with different excitation wavelengths; (c) Excitation (black curve) and emission spectra (red curve) of the n-CDs; Normalized fluorescence intensities (FIs) of n-CDs at (d) different pH values, (e) in NaCl solutions of different concentrations, and (f) after adding various ions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
MTT assay results. (a) Effects of n-CDs at different concentrations on the viability of 4T1 cells for 24 h; (b) and (c) CLSM images of HeLa cells treated at different temperatures. Scale bar: 20 μm.
CLSM images of n-CDs entering the HeLa cells at (a) 5, (b) 10, (c) 20, and (d) 30 min. Scale bar: 20 μm.

Co-localization assays of the n-CDs in living cells. (a) CLSM of nucleolus co-localization assays. (b) CLSM of mitochondria co-localization assays. (c) CLSM of lysosome co-localization assays. Scale bar: 20 μm.

head.ce:title: Novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots with triple targetability as a fluorescent probe for bioimaging of living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Radio Frequency Heterogeneous Integration (Shenzhen University), College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Radio Frequency Heterogeneous Integration (Shenzhen University)
College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering
Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Radio Frequency Heterogeneous Integration (Shenzhen University), College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Investigating the interactions between various organelles can effectively reveal the corresponding biological problems. Currently, studies of organelle interactions typically employ multiple fluorescent probes that can concurrently target different organelles. However, the simultaneous use of multiple probes is complicated to operate, and the probes can interact with each other, affecting the imaging results. Therefore, targeting multiple organelles using a single probe can enhance the process of studying organelle interactions. Carbon dots (CDs), with their abundant surface groups, are expected to solve the abovementioned problems. Herein, we successfully used m-aminophenol and ethylenediamine to prepare high anti-interference capability and triple targetability CDs (n-CDs) with hydrothermal method. The co-localization experiments with commercial probes confirmed that it can target nucleolus, mitochondria and Lysosome at the same time. The preparation of n-CDs provides a new and convenient strategy to study the interaction between various organelles for solving corresponding biological problems, thus revealing the mysteries of life.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Discovered in 2004, CDs have been widely investigated in various fields owing to their unique and advantageous characteristics [1,2]. Notably, CDs have become one of the fluorescent probes used in emerging research topics related to biological problems, owing to their good photobleaching resistance, stable fluorescence properties, no biotoxicity, good biocompatibility, good membrane permeability, abundant surface functional groups, environmentally friendliness, inexpensive raw materials, and simple preparation process [3–9]. Particularly, with the increasing development of biological challenges, and owing to their excellent organelle targeting ability, CDs are of great significance in exploring the life activities of organelles with regard to the occurrence of diseases and drug treatments [10–12].

Because of their abundant surface groups [13–15], CDs have successfully targeted various organelles [16], including mitochondria [17,18], lysosomes [19,20], nuclei [21,22], and endoplasmic reticuli [23]. Though the mechanism is not fully understood, the efficiency of CDs targeting organelles is comparable to that of commercial probes. Therefore, CDs have played an important role in many studies related to the physiological activities of organelles. Mao et al. prepared red fluorescent CDs to target the endoplasmic reticulum [23]. They found that after co-localization of the endoplasmic reticulum with a commercial probe, Pearson's correlation coefficient was 0.9, demonstrating the potential of CDs in endoplasmic reticulum imaging. Gao used p-phenylenediamine and thiourea to prepare lysosome-targeting CDs, with a corresponding overlap coefficient of 0.88. Simultaneously, the CDs exhibited good performance in quantitatively measuring pH [24]; therefore, the effect of pH on lysosomal physiological activities was also investigated in this work. Our group prepared n-CDs to target mitochondria, and imaging showed the n-CDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and excellent ability to target mitochondria, rendering them ideal materials to study mitochondria-related diseases. Furthermore, we realized the detection and visualization of exogenous HS− in mitochondria [25]. The abovementioned studies prove that CDs exhibit excellent performance in targeting organelles; therefore, using CDs as probes has remarkable potential to study the diseases caused by related organelles.

Owing to the complexity and variability of diseases, the investigations of the cause and the treatments of diseases no longer focus on one type of organelle, but on various organelles that require concurrent investigations [26–28]. This poses a significant challenge for studying the interactions of multiple organelles using fluorescent probes, and these complex biological problems need to be addressed. To date, several fluorescent probes have been employed simultaneously to target different organelles to study the interaction between different organelles, revealing the causes of diseases. By using mitochondria, nucleolus, and lysosome-targeting probes to observe organelle interactions, it was found that there is a connection with certain cancers and neurodegenerative diseases [29–33]. Although this method can effectively and intuitively study a variety of cell-cell interactions, selecting the appropriate probe combinations has become a new problem [34–36]. In the simultaneous use of two fluorescent probes targeting different organelles, understanding whether the two probes will interact is essential as this can affect imaging. Furthermore, since the sequence of probe use will affect the imaging, it is necessary to explore the used sequence to ensure the correct study of organelle interactions. This is for probe selection imposed by the interaction between two organelles. However, if the biological problem requires the investigation of three or more target objects, the choice of probe combination is far more complicated.

If only one fluorescent probe that can target various organelles simultaneously is used to achieve the above functions, it can avoid interfering with the imaging is necessary to understand the interaction between organelles [37,38]. Furthermore, this device can enable more intuitive and effective observations, thus revealing the causes of the corresponding biological problems [39–41]. CDs are the most promising fluorescent probes to achieve this goal. Herein, we prepared n-CDs with triple targetability in living cells. The n-CDs exhibited low biotoxicity and good stability, confirmed via co-localization experiments performed using a commercial probe. The results showed that n-CDs can rapidly enter the cell and simultaneously target three different organelles: the nucleolus, lysosomes, and mitochondria. These three types of organelles can be labeled using only the n-CDs, which is of significant importance to the study of biological problems caused by the nucleolus, lysosomes, and mitochondria. This new CDs enables imaging of multiple organelles using only one probe, avoiding the biological toxicity and crosstalk associated with the simultaneous use of multiple probes. Furthermore, this work inspires the preparation of CDs that can effectively target multiple other organelles simultaneously.

The raw materials for the preparation of n-CDs were m-aminophenol and ethylenediamine, purchased from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. Chemicals used to study the stability of n-CDs and other performances were supplied by Macklin (KCl, MnCl2·4H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, AgCl, BaCl2·2H2O, (CH3COO)2Zn, CrCl3·6H2O, CuSO4, NaOH, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, Na2H2PO2, NaBr, NaHCO3, K2CO3, Pb(NO3)2), HCl, Cys, GSH, Hcy and Gln). Commercial colocalization probes were supplied by Biostatus, Red dots-1 for nucleolus, Mito tracker for mitochondria and Lyso tracker for lysosomes. The morphology, chemical structure, and chemical composition of n-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI TECNAI G2 F20, USA), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 5700 spectrometer, USA), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). Optical properties of each sample were characterized using ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis, UV-2550 Shimadzu, Japan) and fluorescence spectroscopy (PL, Varian Cary Eclipse Agilent, USA). The cell imaging was performed via laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscopy (CLSM, Nikon A1R MP+, Japan).

To prepare the initial product of n-CDs, 0.2 g of m-aminophenol, 200 μL of ethylenediamine, and 15 mL of ultrapure water were added to a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave remained in a drying cabinet at 180 °C for 24 h. The initial n-CDs product was prepared after it was cooled to 25 °C. Then, the initial n-CDs product was added to a dialysis bag and submerged in ultrapure water for 3 d. Finally, the n-CDs solution was obtained. (Various raw material ratios, different temperatures and different reaction times have been tried. This synthesis method was the optimal condition obtained by the control variable method.)

The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was employed to study the cytotoxicity of n-CDs. First, the HeLa cells were cultured in a 96-well plate and placed in a cell incubator for 24 h. Then, different concentrations of the n-CDs solutions (blank, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL) were added to the 96-well plate and cultured for 48 h. Finally, the cytotoxicity data of n-CDs solutions were obtained, and all experiments were repeated more than thrice.

First, 20 μL of the n-CDs solutions (20 μg/mL) were added to the cell culture plate and incubated in the cell culture chamber for 20 min. Then, the cell culture plate was washed with phosphate-buffered saline. Sample preparation was considered complete after three repetitions. Finally, the cell samples were placed on the stage of a Nikon A1R MP + laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope, and the confocal images of living HeLa cells were acquired at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

First, 20 μL of the n-CDs solutions (20 μg/mL) were added to the cell culture plate and incubated in the cell culture chamber for 20 min and then 200 nM of a commercial probe (Red dots-1 for nucleolus, Mito tracker for mitochondria and Lyso tracke) for another 40 min, respectively. Then, the cell culture plate was washed with phosphate-buffered saline. Sample preparation was considered complete after three repetitions. Finally, the cell samples were placed on the stage of a Nikon A1R MP + laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope, and the confocal images of living HeLa cells were acquired at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm and 561 nm for n-CDs and commercial probe, respectively.

Abundant surface groups are essential for the CD-targeting of various organelles; therefore, systematically characterizing the structure of n-CDs is necessary. The particle size and distribution of n-CDs are uniform, as seen from TEM images in Fig. 1 (a). Fig. 1(b) shows the particle size distribution of 100 n-CDs particles in TEM image, and the results indicate that the particle size of n-CDs is extremely small (2.0–6.0 nm), and the smaller size affords easy access to living cells. Moreover, the surface charge of n-CDs is also crucial for cell research. As is illustrated in the Sup. 1, it can be observed that the zeta potential of n-CDs is −47.5 mV. This indicates a negatively charged surface, and the relatively high potential enables it to maintain excellent dispersion stability. Undoubtedly, this is a favorable characteristic for cellular imaging [42,43]. The surface groups and elements of the n-CDs were also studied. The FT-IR spectrum can be characterized systematically for surface groups of n-CDs (Fig. 1(c)). Several characteristic bands shown at 3310, 1623, 1411, and 1392 cm−1 correspond to C–OH, CO, C–N, and C–O, respectively [37,38]. Additionally, the XPS spectrum showed three peaks (C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s) [39–41], confirming the presence of C, N, and O elements (Fig. 1(d)) in the n-CDs. The high-resolution spectra displayed the more specific structures of n-CDs. Accordingly, four peaks were observed in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(e)), viz. C–C/CC (284.1 eV), C–N (285.6 eV), C–O (286.2 eV), and CO (288.2 eV). In addition, the N 1s spectrum of the n-CDs exhibited three distinct N structure types, viz. C–NC (399.1 eV), C–N (400.2 eV), and N–H (401.8 eV), as shown in Fig. 1(f) n-CDs have a good ability to target various organelles, owing to these abundant surface groups.

After determining the structure of n-CDs, their optical properties and stability were investigated. First, the optical properties of n-CDs were systematically characterized. In Fig. 2 (a), the three-dimensional excitation-emission spectra fully display the range of excitation and emission wavelengths for n-CDs, which are 325–470 and 405–620 nm, respectively. The centers of the excitation (black curve) and emission wavelengths (red curve) were 411 and 512 nm, respectively (Fig. 2(b)). The insert of Fig. 2(b) shows the n-CDs solution images under sunlight and UV lamps. Additionally, the UV–Vis (Fig. 2(c)) spectrum of the n-CDs exhibited two absorption peaks at 236 and 288 nm, which can be attributed to the π–π∗ and n–π∗ transitions, respectively [42]. Then, the optical stability of n-CDs in different environments was studied. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the fluorescence intensity (FI) of n-CDs did not significantly change in the pH range of 4.0–9.0, which covers the range of pH in living cells. In Fig. 2(e), the FI of n-CDs did not significantly change with varying concentrations of NaCl. Moreover, the concentrations studied significantly exceeded the normal concentrations of NaCl in living cells. Simultaneously, the effects of different ions on the FI of n-CDs were also studied (Fig. 2(f)). Results indicated that different ions did not affect the FI of n-CDs. Notably, the species of ions studied significantly exceeded the species of ions in living cells. In addition, the effects of common biomolecules (Cys, GSH, Hcy, GIn) substances on n-CDs have been studied, and n-CDs likewise exhibit stable optical properties (Sup. 2). The fluorescence decay spectrum of n-CDs is presented in Sup. 3, from which a dual-exponential fitting reveals an average fluorescence lifetime of 1.10 ns. This observation underscores its remarkably short fluorescence lifetime, thereby endowing it with excellent temporal resolution for imaging experiments. The abovementioned results indicate that the n-CDs exhibit good optical stability and are suitable for use as a fluorescent probe in the imaging of living cells.

Following structural and optical characterizations, n-CDs were investigated as a multi-organelle-targeting fluorescent probe in living cells. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity and uptake patterns of the n-CDs were investigated. First, the cytotoxicity of n-CDs was studied via MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the activity of the cells decreased slightly with the change in concentration of n-CDs. HeLa cells were incubated with n-CDs at a concentration of 200 μg/mL for 24 h. The cell activity was more than 92 %, which is ten times the concentration of cell imaging, indicating that n-CDs exhibit very low cytotoxicity. As shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c), compared to that of living cells at 37 °C, the FI of n-CDs slightly declined at 4 °C, indicating that n-CDs can enter the cell via free diffusion, and that cell absorption of n-CDs is also related to energy. These results indicate that smaller particle sizes of n-CDs can be easily used as fluorescent probes into living cells.

The process of n-CDs directly entering cells determines the results of live-cell imaging. Therefore, to obtain the best cell imaging results, determining the optimal time for the n-CDs to enter the cell is necessary. Fig. 4 (a) shows an image of the cell 5 min after the n-CDs were introduced into it. However, the bright fluorescence signal and clear organelle structure were not observed, owing to the low incubation time. Fewer n-CDs entered the cell, resulting in poor imaging results. Therefore, the incubation times of n-CDs were increased to 10 and 20 min, as shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c), respectively. After 10 min of incubation, the organelle structure was clearer than that of 5 min. After 20 min of incubation, the image quality was significantly better than that of 10 min. Therefore, increasing the incubation time may further improve the image quality. As shown in Fig. 4(d), after 30 min of incubation, the results of cell imaging were almost identical to those of after the 20 min incubation. Therefore, 20 min was the optimal incubation time for cells incubated with n-CDs.

After obtaining clear cell images and determining the optimal incubation time and concentration of n-CDs, the organelles targeted by n-CDs were investigated. Commercial probes were selected to incubate cells with n-CDs to determine the organelles targeted by the n-CDs. The cell images show that the n-CDs are mainly labeled in the nucleus and cytoplasm. Therefore, commercial nucleolar probes (Red dots–1) were selected to determine the ability of n-CDs to target the nucleolus, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The fluorescence of Red dots–1 and n-CDs coincided well at the nucleolus of cells, indicating that n-CDs have a good nucleolar targeting ability. Subsequently, to verify the organelle targeted by the n-CDs in the cytoplasm, commercial mitochondrial (MitoTracker) and lysosomal (LysoTracker) probes were selected for co-localization experiments. Fig. 5(b) and (c) show the n-CDs incubated with MitoTracker and LysoTracker, respectively. Both commercial probes showed a high degree of targeting overlap, indicating that the n-CDs exhibited good mitochondria and lysosomes targeting abilities. Thus, n-CDs can be used as fluorescent probes targeting three types of organelles (nucleolus, mitochondria, and lysosomes) simultaneously to study the interaction mechanisms of subcellular structures in living cells.

In this study, we used a one-step hydrothermal treatment method with m-aminophenol and ethylenediamine to synthesize three-target n-CDs. The n-CDs exhibited good optical stability and extremely low biotoxicity, which resulted in an excellent fluorescent probe for live-cell imaging. Furthermore, their targeting ability was verified via co-localization experiments using three commercial probes. The results indicated that n-CDs can target the nucleolus, mitochondria, and lysosomes simultaneously in living cells. In addition, n-CDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for studying the interaction between the nucleolus, mitochondria, and lysosomes. Moreover, the n-CDs provide a new strategy for studying the interactions between various organelles. Single, multi-target targeting probes have broad applications in the study of complex biological problems, providing critical information for solving the corresponding biological problems and overcoming the mysteries of the microscopic world.

Jiaqing Guo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yu Chen: Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaoyu Weng: Validation. Yiping Wang: Visualization. Junle Qu: Supervision, Software. Jun Song: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Liwei Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no potential conflicts of interest with respect to authorship and/or publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been partially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFF0502900); National Natural Science Foundation of China (62275166/62175161/61835009/62127819/62022059/62405201), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M712164). Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2023A1515011114/2024A1515011800/2024A1515010999), Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (JCYJ20190808160207366/JCYJ20210324095810028/JCYJ20210324095613036/JCYJ20190808151215399) and Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Photonics and Biophotonics (ZDSYS20210623092006020). We thank Instrument Analysis Center of Shenzhen University, Photonics Research Center of Shenzhen University, and Ph.D. Lin for the technical assistance with the Leica SP8 confocal microscopy instrument.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of yellow-green-emissive carbon dots and their application in constructing a fluorescent turn-on nanoprobe for imaging of selenol in living cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red-Emissive carbon dots for fluorescent, photoacoustic, and thermal theranostics in living mice

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-targeting fluorescent carbon dots for diagnosis of brain cancer cells
